speaker: interviewee
text: The silver, gold medal, you don't hear these days this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You do.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to my grandparents' 50th wedding anniversary. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mail?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mailman.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm glad he was now.

speaker: interviewee
text: He usually comes 4:00 o'clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think he came today on purpose to interrupt. How often do you see-- you live so far from me. It's like 45 minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we're very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: But do you see him all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Him and his brother, yeah. We-- the only--

speaker: interviewee
text: First tape, Fela Gipsman, Peter [? Hamberger ?] on the camera.

speaker: interviewer
text: First of all, could you please tell us your full name, where you were born, and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Fela Gipsman. I'm born in Poland, in Bedzin, September 5, 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me a little bit about your life, what life was like in Bedzin when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: And about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- I was fortunate. I had a very good life. We were well-to-do. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had my parents, and I had three brothers, one older, one two years, then it was me, and two younger brothers. We were all two years apart. We were able to go to private schools and public schools. And then, of course, before, I went to a private kindergarten, which I have pictures to show you later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your parents, what they did?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was traveling a lot because we had-- how should I say-- a wholesale place that he was sending home boxcars with merchandise. And that wholesale place was to sell food for cattle. And then we also had the oil factory with my uncle and grandfather. Together, that-- there was-- that oil factory, there were a lot of people working day and night. And that oil that we were producing, other factories were buying to make for painting. I don't know how you call this. Oil for painting. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my father traveled a lot. My mother helped out in the business. And we had a live-in maid and a bookkeeper, telephone. So we were OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Jewish-- what was your Jewish life like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I went to a public school every day. And after public school, I went to Hebrew school every day. And my brothers went to chaida after school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me a little bit about what Jewish life was like in your community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most were Jewish people living in the community, and we were traditional Jews. We were just observing holidays. My father had a seat in the shul of course, with his name on it. And we were Jews observing the holidays, not Orthodox Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you also tell me [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a little bit about your day-to-day life as you were growing up in Bedzin, what life was like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My life was good, just was-- with my girlfriends, you know, going to school. And, and I have no, no complaints.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, but if you could give us a little description about your school, about life with your brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was good. I was the only girl with three brothers. I was very spoiled at home. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were a very close-knit family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember or can you describe the first signs of danger or anti-Semitism that you actually experienced as a-- growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not experience-- not that I know of. I was just going to school every day, so I really didn't feel anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was, was it that you felt pretty-- like you were almost in a cocoon, that you were fairly protected? Is that what you mean, where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the life was nice, mostly Jews, and, well, a lot of Polish people, too. But I went to school, and, and I mind my own business. I was too busy with school, and I don't think my parents experienced anything. Not that I know of.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit-- now, can you tell me how you remember the first signs of danger as the war was beginning to come to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were talking about that Hitler's going to invade Poland. And he did invade very fast because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1939, when Hitler came in, he just crossed the border, and all the Polish people, the soldiers, were running away. So that was very fast. And when he came in, I remember this like today that they-- when we had to wear the Jewish armband-- and they-- around us. And I didn't have one on me, and they took me down where the Polish soldiers used to be in those [NON-ENGLISH], in those big buildings where they lived. And they took me down there, and they brought a lot of people that day, young and old, who didn't wear the armbands. And we were-- had to stay on our knees all night long because we did not wear that armband. And I was so scared and crying all night long. And the next morning, they let us go home. That was my first experience with the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what followed?

speaker: interviewee
text: What followed? That was the end of '39 because they came in September to Bedzin, the Germans. After this, we couldn't go to school anymore. We had to have a certain time that we cannot go out at night anymore. And times were very bad with food. And I couldn't see my friends, you know. We were just like-- everybody was cooped up in the house. And 1942 in December, they knocked on our door in the middle of the night, and they had to have a certain amount of girls to take to camp. And they knocked on my door, and they wanted me to go with them. And I'll never forget this that my father was crying. [SOBBING] He wanted to give them money to leave me home, but they wouldn't listen. They just took me away. And that was-- and since they send me to camp that time, my three brothers were left with my parents that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they continuing, at this time, this house in which you lived-- was this the house in which you had always lived? Or were you-- had you been moved into a ghetto area at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the ghetto happened after, not at my time.

speaker: interviewer
text: The ghetto occurred after you were taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think the ghetto occurred in end of 19-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 1939, '43, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Germans came for you, what was that they-- why did they want-- where did they want to take you? Did they give any explanation?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just need a certain amount of girls to go and work at camps, you know. That's why they're rounding up one time, girls, one time, boys, one time, men. They had certain orders to do what to round up and when. That's how it was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after they took you, what happened? Was it in the night? Was it in the day? When did-- how did this happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: They sent me to next town's [NON-ENGLISH]. They sent me. And that was like-- they used to call it the [NON-ENGLISH]. Like, they're putting people together and sending them out different places. And I was there, I think, a day and a night. And they had a certain amount of girls they sent to Blechhammer. Blechhammer was that time a concentration camp already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they combined girls from that camp Blechhammer and the girls who were shipped there. And they combined hundred girls, and they send us with boxcars to Sudetenland. And that was deep Czechoslovakia. And we were only a hundred girls in that camp. Now, can you tell me, when you first arrived in Blechhammer, what exactly-- can you tell me what you witnessed and what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. It was just one room, waiting to be sent out. I didn't witness anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you were taken to the Sudetenland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the camp they took you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schatzlar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Schatzlar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this was a small camp. It was belonging like to Theresienstadt. All the small camps belong to a big camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a satellite camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And while you were there, when you arrived, what happened? Can you tell me?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I had a little diamond ring that my parents gave me. First of all, they took it away right away. And they let us wear our clothes. That was a camp that we didn't wear those striped clothes. And we were assigned work in a [NON-ENGLISH] factory. This is in a factory that you are working on raw material for-- how you call this? Thread. Yeah. The factory-- the machine was from here all the way further than the kitchen, and one had to work on this machine. It was mostly the spools were running like an old-- you had to hold it, but you had to stop it if the thread tore. And we were working barefoot. It was all wet on the concrete. And then they gave us those wooden shoes to wear. And we went every day to this factory to work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was, was it all just women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was Czech woman. But a few were spying on the Czech woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a picture from the Czech woman that she helped me with a sandwich every day because I made some slippers, you know, from the thread and different things I was crocheting.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you bartered-- It was like an exchange. You gave her something, and she--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I just made it for her. And she used to-- I used to go to the bathroom, and she used to tell me news, what's going on. And then she used to give me a sandwich, and later it got so bad, because other Czech was spying on her. So she used to hide the sandwich. She used to come and leave the sandwich under my machine so I have something to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you-- you got-- were you-- what, what was every, a day like when you-- how were you woken up and at what time? And what-- can you take us through the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to get up 5:00 in the morning and you know, have some-- they gave us something to eat. And they gave us-- I don't know how you call this in English. It looks like radish, but it's big and yellow. I don't know the names of it. And we used to eat this for breakfast. And I had a lot of problems with my stomach. I couldn't digest it. So for the first year, they gave me, like, oatmeal every morning. And like, I used to get the sandwiches from my parents once a week in a little bag. After a year, they took this away from me, too. And later on, I had no choice. I had to eat it. I had a lot of problems with my stomach.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that your parents sent you sandwiches. How-- can you explain this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was dry, two pieces of toast, like dry bread, two pieces.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how could your parents-- how did your parents manage to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not in the ghetto that time. They were still living at home the first year. Like I said, the ghetto probably started in '43, later on. And I was sent away '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they managed-- you wrote them letters, and you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --were able to correspond?

speaker: interviewee
text: Letters, just a couple words, you know. Like, mail used to come in to us, too. But then just the first year. That was it. It was not a concentration camp that time. It was like they were calling a working camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after-- getting back to the way the day went-- after you were given some breakfast, then what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, we went to-- we marched to the factory and worked there all day long. And then, at night, we came back. We got dinner. It was just like soup with-- they used to call [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know how you call this in, in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: With some hard bread? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, hard bread. And when it came Christmas around, we used to get dog biscuits. And that was like the best cookie in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you-- what happened from this camp? Where were you-- what was-- where were you taken? Where were you shipped?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was two and a half years there till I was liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were never anywhere else?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you witnessed-- so how would you describe, in comparison to other people and other survivors what it was like where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very bad. We thought that's the worst. But comparing what I heard later on, it was pretty good.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were there, did you know what was going on with your parents? How did-- well, how did you get news?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. The woman was just telling me about the war news, where the Germans are, where the Russians are. That's all, when I was-- the one who gave me the sandwich. That's all. We didn't know anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- did you witness while you were there-- were women shipped out to other places? Or were all the women pretty much kept right at this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Everybody was kept there. We were 100 women to start with at camp. Then, they brought in 20 more girls from Hungary. And then they get 20 more girls from Hungary, so we were 120 girls altogether. And I was-- five girls, including myself, we were the youngest girls in the camp. They called us the kindergarten.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there any other, any other girls from Bedzin with you at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Oh, who-- as, as the war progressed, can you tell me what-- you'd said that you-- what news you got, you got from the woman, the Czech woman. But as, as the tide turned on the war, what were you then expecting? Who liberated you? And what was, what was it like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The last day that was the last day for us in camp, you know, we knew that the Americans are close and the Russians are close. And, and the day before it ended, they opened up the camps. And all the SS men run away. But I tell you, before it was over, we had to march every day and dig ditches for the German army that they can shoot out, a [NON-ENGLISH], how do you call this?

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had trenches?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trenches.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. We had to dig those very deep down. And so the German army could go down and shoot out. And that time, Auschwitz was already over, so all the SS men with their dogs came to our camps, not just our camps, but all the camps around. And when we were digging the ditches, the dogs were running around. And we were not supposed to even stand up or straighten out. We were so frightened and digging the ditches all day long. That was just before the war was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe for me the day of liberation for you? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that morning, all the SS men run away with the dogs, and they opened the doors. We ran out on the street. And we were waiting who was going to come first, Americans or the Russians. And the Russians came in first. And they liberated the town. And, luckily, it was a Jewish, a Jewish general who liberated the camp with his platoon, whatever. And he was there in the camp watching over us that no Russian soldier should touch us. That was very lucky for us. And they opened up all the stores the next day for us. We should take whatever we want to. So the only thing we were looking for was food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about clothing? What had-- what did- what king of clothing had they--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was wearing clothes. What I-- you know, I didn't wear those striped clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ever?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Never. And I had a number wearing on my neck. Everybody had a number.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in this camp, were there any kind of medical checkups and things where they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. A dentist came from the bigger camps once in six months and so did the doctor. But nobody in this camp ever had their period, because they were putting something in the food. So that was it. And after the liberation, it was quite a while then until everything back to normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they-- did they, at any point during the two and a half years you spent there, were women-- any of the women taken, any of the women taken away and transported or shipped out to someplace else?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you all Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, all Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they-- it was as if it was a protective enclave. I mean, in, in regards to everyplace else.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we supposed to be shipped to Auschwitz maybe six months before the war ended. And those boxcars were ready for us. But the, the Russians were moving so fast that they didn't have a chance. Because if we would have been shipped, I wouldn't be here today.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once liberation came, can you tell me what, what happened to you at that point, where you went, what you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went with my friends. We stayed a week or two in the place there. And after this, we went to Czechoslovakia to-- not far from Prague. And we were there on a farm, all of us together, and just working for food. And then a sister of my friend went back to Poland looking for family. And that must have been in the Jewish center that everybody was looking for somebody, that my cousin, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] brother was there. And he had been to talk to her, and she told him that she's living with me near Czechoslovakia. And so he gave her a message that I should come to them and be with them. And if I won't come to him, he'll come and get me. That was the message.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I just had some underwear, little small suitcase, and I went to Poland to look for him. And he was living in Breslau with my friends. And that was my future husband at the time. I didn't know. But he was a schoolmate of theirs, and we were all living together. When I came there with the small suitcase, my cousin took my suitcase. And they lived in an apartment. And he opened up the suitcase, and I had some underwear. And I don't even remember if something to change or not. He threw everything out through the window. He told me I'll give you better things than that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I lived with them till my uncle found out that we are alive, and he came to Breslau and tried to smuggle us to Germany because all my uncles lived in Germany. I had four uncles which survived. And they were mine-- they were brothers of my mother. He smuggled us through. Once they caught us on the border, the Russians. We went back to Prague that time. And then he-- on foot. We had to go at night. And then he smuggled us again through, and we made it. And then we came to Munich. I lived with my uncles until I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then how long were you in, in Germany before you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: From '45 till-- 1949, I came to the United States. I got married. I lived in Munich from '45 to '47. '47, I got married and moved to Stuttgart, where my husband's father lived there. And '47 to '49, I lived there. And '49, we came to the United States. We registered to Australia, to any place that we could leave Germany. And the papers went so fast, you know, that, within three months from registering to the United States, we went to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you arrive in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived at Munich. I mean-- sorry. We arrived to New York. And we had big tags hanging around us and the names. And right away from the station, New York, they transferred us to Lancaster, Pennsylvania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why you were sent to Lancaster?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because every city sponsored so many people. And we were from the Joint United Distributions, whatever they're called. And that city sponsored us. And we were the first couple from after the war to arrive to Lancaster, Pennsylvania. And so a woman was waiting for us with the car there in Lancaster. And she picked us up and took us to furnished apartment, Jewish people. And they provided us with a furnished room and food. And they were kind-- there were only 400 Jews living in this town at that time, German Jews, I think. And they came there, and they wanted to meet us. They thought that people coming, they uneducated with-- like years ago, with the bag on their back. So they were disappointed in a way. But, anyway, they were happy to see us, and they are the ones who sponsored us to come to the United States. They were responsible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was life like after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they treated us very well. They gave us food and a nice place to live with Jewish people. They-- every couple took us out every night someplace else. And they-- and we wanted to go to work. We didn't want to stay home. And they told us, don't worry, in the United States, you'll work plenty. Don't rush. I said we don't want to-- we're young. We want to go to work. So I found a job in a factory that they were sewing children's dresses. And my husband went to work in a cigar factory. So we're making $0.70 an hour. At that time, the bread was $0.22. So when we went shopping from $10, it was enough to eat for us for more than a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were staying only three months there. After this, my husband found out, he has a cousin in Detroit. So we went to Detroit because we wanted to be with family together. So they were sad to see us go. But we left there, and we came to Detroit. And in Detroit, we lived four years. And what happened in Detroit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my husband probably told you the story already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you can tell us a little bit if you like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We lived in Detroit. And he worked in automobile factory. And, and then the factory went on strike for 90 days. And that was very bad. That time, I got pregnant. I had a little boy and--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in 1950, February 23, a day after Washington's birthday. And I got very sick because, apparently, the doctors didn't do such a good job on me. And I had the best doctor, Jewish doctor, but I hemorrhaged a lot. And I was laying in blood, like in a bathtub up to my neck. And my neighbors came up and put the chairs under the bed. I should lay with my feet up high. Then, the doctor came, and he gave me shots. And so everything should come out. For a whole year, I had to go for shots, iron shots. And I had to drink Ovaltine. And the first three months, I didn't handle the baby because I was too weak. So my husband took care of him. And after that, I was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then after Detroit, what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: After Detroit, my neighbor was going in-- he had a friend living in Los Angeles. And he wrote to him that the climate is so good, you can get work, why he doesn't come here. And we lived in the same building, so he talked me into come, too. And we already had a nice furnished apartment and friends, and, and I decided. I said, OK, a better climate, we'll go because the winters were very hard. By the time you dressed up, you had to go back up to the room.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, OK, we'll go with you. So, one day, my husband comes home from work. And I sold all the furniture. And he had no place where to sleep. I sold the bed, too. And I said, we're going. So my friend said, you're crazy. You have no-- you don't have anybody. Where are you going? And I said, we'll manage. So we started two cars in the morning, and it took us five and a half days to come to Los Angeles. And my friend got lost on the road because his motor burned down. So we waited for him a whole day. We didn't see him, so we decided to continue on our own. And we came here, and we looked up his friend. And we told him what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: We rented a furnished room on Pico. Near Sears, it used to be. And we lived there for quite a while because we wanted to rent a place. And wherever we went, they saw a little boy, they closed the door in front of us. So they didn't want to rent that time with children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, we found a place after that. And then my friend came, and it was very bad. My husband was going every morning, got up early to look for work. I couldn't work that time because he was a little boy. He was three years old when we came here from Detroit. And he was knocking door to door until he found a job. And then you-- what happened?
